sourceSectionFilterAuditRows.sectionTitle: Jasper Maynard Zhang : Jasper Maynard Zhang, Informatics: "I am interested in how technology and data science can be used to address causes I care about." Read more.
sourceSectionFilterAuditRows.rawLine: Informatics courses can also be understood as informing a variety of focus areas, which are lenses that frame different considerations of information in the curriculum. For example, Informatics offers courses in data science (INFO 37x/47x), cybersecurity (INFO 31x/41x), and search and retrieval (INFO 32x/42x). Most Informatics courses can be labeled as drawing on one or more focus areas; students can use these focus areas to help plan their core classes and electives. See the full list of focus areas for details.

sourceSectionFilterAuditRows.sectionTitle: Autumn 2026
sourceSectionFilterAuditRows.rawLine: AI-powered development tools have made it possible to design, build, and iterate on real technology solutions faster than ever before — opening new opportunities for organizations like nonprofits that have historically lacked access to technical resources. This capstone takes advantage of that shift: working with real nonprofit clients on defined problems, students will use current vibe coding tools to build and deliver working systems, transitioning their management to the organizations they serve and freeing them to focus on their missions and impact. Along the way, students will develop and refine a methodology for doing so with rigor, judgment, and craft.

sourceSectionFilterAuditRows.sectionTitle: Autumn 2026
sourceSectionFilterAuditRows.rawLine: Explores how information flows through the construction industry, with emphasis on the data, coordination, and contractual requirements that shape design and execution in large projects. Students examine how construction documents, subcontractor commitments, project communications, and site realities interact across the lifecycle of a project. Topics include access to proprietary and public project data, synthetic data generation, interviews with industry practitioners, and the role of AI agents in supporting collaboration between subcontractors, general contractors, and design teams. Through site visits, field observation, and applied research, students investigate how informatics can improve decision-making, communication, and compliance in construction settings.

sourceSectionFilterAuditRows.sectionTitle: This recurring, quarterly research studio will engage you as a volunteer open source contributor that helps design, build, and maintain Wordplay, creative coding research platform for creating accessible, interactive typographic media. Our research goals are to create a global platform for creative expression with language that celebrates every individual's culture, identity, and values, while also enabling youth to learn about the power and limits of data and algorithms. Your contributions can include designing and redesigning the language and platform, implementing new user interface features, improving correctness and reliability of current features, localizing to one or more of the world's languages, writing automated tests, creating Wordplay examples, verifying accessibility, and teaching your peers. As a studio course, there will be no lectures, only community-based making, collaboration, coordination, and communication. Can be taken multiple times, and volunteer work can continue beyond the quarter.

Our research goals are to create a global platform for creative expression with language that celebrates every individual's culture, identity, and values, while also enabling youth to learn about the power and limits of data and algorithms. Your contributions can include designing and redesigning the language and platform, implementing new user interface features, improving correctness and reliability of current features, localizing to one or more of the world's languages, writing automated tests, creating Wordplay examples, verifying accessibility, and teaching your peers. As a studio course, there will be no lectures, only community-based making, collaboration, coordination, and communication. Can be taken multiple times, and volunteer work can continue beyond the quarter. sourceSectionFilterAuditRows.rawLine: Community-Driven HCI gives students the opportunity to explore how technology can be designed with local communities, while at the same time engaging broader questions in Human-Computer Interaction for Development (HCI4D), as well as notions of Autonomous Design. Students will pursue quarter-long design projects situated in Seattle. In addition to introducing participatory design methods, the course will also encourage students to think critically about issues such as equity, sustainability, and other related community-led forms of sociotechnical practice, specifically within the context of underserved or underrepresented communities both locally and abroad.

sourceSectionFilterAuditRows.sectionTitle: The course will start with several weeks of game design basics, including mechanics, dynamics, “fun”, visual and UX design for games, narrative, user research for games, and an overview of game genres. We’ll then cover various aspects of games for social good, including game-based learning, social awareness games, social action games, and social engagement games in a variety of categories. We will also cover topics such as toxicity in multiplayer games, representation, and more. Assignments will focus on team creation more than theory and we will have a final team project where you create a game demo or prototype.

sourceSectionFilterAuditRows.rawLine: In this course students critically examine current social media platforms and then design alternative platform concepts grounded in lived experience, youth perspectives, and explicit values. Rather than treating social media as either simply harmful or simply empowering, the course asks students to identify the specific design choices, incentives, metrics, and social contexts that shape how platforms affect connection, identity, intimacy, attention, safety, belonging, and agency.

sourceSectionFilterAuditRows.rawLine: Designed for INFO majors who are interested in startups and entrepreneurship, this course will cover how new companies are founded, managed, funded, and grown. Through readings, guest speakers, activities, and case studies, students will learn what life is like as a startup founder and how to navigate the inevitable challenges that arise, as well as the mechanics of raising money for a startup through both equity and non-dilutive funding options. While the course material is applicable to many different types of startups, there will be a particular emphasis on social impact startups, SaaS companies, and online marketplaces. Developers, designers, and product managers who are thinking about launching a startup at some point in their career, as well as those interested in joining an early-stage startup after graduation, are encouraged to enroll in the course.

sourceSectionFilterAuditRows.rawLine: Examines theoretical and practical questions and challenges inherent in designing, building, deploying, maintaining, and regulating trustworthy and safe sociotechnical systems. Topics include: online governance and the emergence of Trust & Safety; an introduction to attack surfaces; reactively and proactively defining, identifying, and mitigating harms (e.g., hate speech, fraud, extremist content, child sexual abuse materials, platform manipulation); the increasing role of regulation and compliance; the challenges of “trust” and “safety” at scale; the threats and opportunities of emerging technologies; and careers in Trust & Safety.

sourceSectionFilterAuditRows.rawLine: Nonprofits frequently face constraints in budget and technical expertise, limiting their ability to implement and manage sophisticated technology solutions. These solutions could significantly enhance engagement with their beneficiaries, efficiently handle constituent management, and automate repetitive tasks. The class aims to work with a nonprofit sponsor to identify high-value workflows that can be automated and utilize no code and AI solutions for designing, developing, and testing these workflows. Our goal is to deploy these solutions and transition their management to nonprofits, unburdening them from menial tasks and freeing up resources to focus on their missions and impact.

sourceSectionFilterAuditRows.sectionTitle: This recurring, quarterly research studio will engage you as a volunteer open source contributor that helps design, build, and maintain Wordplay, creative coding research platform for creating accessible, interactive typographic media. Our research goals are to create a global platform for creative expression with language that celebrates every individual's culture, identity, and values, while also enabling youth to learn about the power and limits of data and algorithms. Your contributions can include designing and redesigning the language and platform, implementing new user interface features, improving correctness and reliability of current features, localizing to one or more of the world's languages, writing automated tests, creating Wordplay examples, verifying accessibility, and teaching your peers. As a studio course, there will be no lectures, only community-based making, collaboration, coordination, and communication. Can be taken multiple times, and volunteer work can continue beyond the quarter.

sourceSectionFilterAuditRows.sectionTitle: In this course you will design and create a video game for social good. “Social good” games include game-based learning, social awareness games, social action games, and social engagement games in a variety of categories. Over the course of 10 weeks, you will work in teams to come up with a strong idea for a game with your team and then design the game, the aesthetics, the story, the game mechanics and create a working one-level working version of the game. You will research your audience and subject area, ideate and test ideas, create prototypes, playtest your game and get feedback.

sourceSectionFilterAuditRows.sectionTitle: Examines artificial intelligence technologies in the context of literary and cultural histories of text reuse. Explores concepts including intellectual property, authorship, parody, and adaptation, drawing on approaches from literary studies and data science. Considers impacts of data and computation on textual production and analysis.

sourceSectionFilterAuditRows.rawLine: Designed for Informatics majors who are interested in startups and entrepreneurship, this course will cover how new companies are founded, managed, funded, and grown. Through readings, guest speakers, activities, and case studies, students will learn what life is like as a startup founder and how to navigate the inevitable challenges that arise, as well as the mechanics of raising money for a startup through both equity and non-dilutive funding options. While the course material is applicable to many different types of startups, there will be a particular emphasis on social impact startups, SaaS companies, and online marketplaces. Developers, designers, and product managers who are thinking about launching a startup at some point in their career, as well as those interested in joining an early-stage startup after graduation, are encouraged to enroll in the course.

sourceSectionFilterAuditRows.sectionTitle: This recurring, quarterly research studio will engage you as a volunteer open source contributor that helps design, build, and maintain Wordplay, creative coding research platform for creating accessible, interactive typographic media. Our research goals are to create a global platform for creative expression with language that celebrates every individual's culture, identity, and values, while also enabling youth to learn about the power and limits of data and algorithms. Your contributions can include designing and redesigning the language and platform, implementing new user interface features, improving correctness and reliability of current features, localizing to one or more of the world's languages, writing automated tests, creating Wordplay examples, verifying accessibility, and teaching your peers. As a studio course, there will be no lectures, only community-based making, collaboration, coordination, and communication. Can be taken multiple times, and volunteer work can continue beyond the quarter.

sourceSectionFilterAuditRows.rawLine: This course focuses on UX for games in depth. The first part of the course contains deep dives into various aspects of UX design in general: UX design, UI design, Visual design, and Game design. Then, the course will focus on the practice of creating components of the game user experience including maps, inventory, onboarding, sound design, and more. We will end will topics on accessibility, mobile, and console UX design.

We will end will topics on accessibility, mobile, and console UX design. Our research goals are to create a global platform for creative expression with language that celebrates every individual's culture, identity, and values, while also enabling youth to learn about the power and limits of data and algorithms. Your contributions can include designing and redesigning the language and platform, implementing new user interface features, improving correctness and reliability of current features, localizing to one or more of the world's languages, writing automated tests, creating Wordplay examples, verifying accessibility, and teaching your peers. As a studio course, there will be no lectures, only community-based making, collaboration, coordination, and communication. Can be taken multiple times, and volunteer work can continue beyond the quarter.

Read more. Raw line: Informatics courses can also be understood as informing a variety of focus areas, which are lenses that frame different considerations of information in the curriculum. For example, Informatics offers courses in data science (INFO 37x/47x), cybersecurity (INFO 31x/41x), and search and retrieval (INFO 32x/42x). Most Informatics courses can be labeled as drawing on one or more focus areas; students can use these focus areas to help plan their core classes and electives. [parser prerequisite filter audit] Owner id: uw-seattle-informatics Source URL: https://ischool.uw.edu/programs/informatics/curriculum Section title: Autumn 2026 Raw line: AI-powered development tools have made it possible to design, build, and iterate on real technology solutions faster than ever before — opening new opportunities for organizations like nonprofits that have historically lacked access to technical resources. This capstone takes advantage of that shift: working with real nonprofit clients on defined problems, students will use current vibe coding tools to build and deliver working systems, transitioning their management to the organizations they serve and freeing them to focus on their missions and impact. Along the way, students will develop and refine a methodology for doing so with rigor, judgment, and craft. [parser prerequisite filter audit] Owner id: uw-seattle-informatics Source URL: https://ischool.uw.edu/programs/informatics/curriculum Section title: Autumn 2026 Raw line: Explores how information flows through the construction industry, with emphasis on the data, coordination, and contractual requirements that shape design and execution in large projects. Students examine how construction documents, subcontractor commitments, project communications, and site realities interact across the lifecycle of a project. Topics include access to proprietary and public project data, synthetic data generation, interviews with industry practitioners, and the role of AI agents in supporting collaboration between subcontractors, general contractors, and design teams. Through site visits, field observation, and applied research, students investigate how informatics can improve decision-making, communication, and compliance in construction settings. [parser prerequisite filter audit] Owner id: uw-seattle-informatics Source URL: https://ischool.uw.edu/programs/informatics/curriculum Section title: This recurring, quarterly research studio will engage you as a volunteer open source contributor that helps design, build, and maintain Wordplay, creative coding research platform for creating accessible, interactive typographic media. Our research goals are to create a global platform for creative expression with language that celebrates every individual's culture, identity, and values, while also enabling youth to learn about the power and limits of data and algorithms. Your contributions can include designing and redesigning the language and platform, implementing new user interface features, improving correctness and reliability of current features, localizing to one or more of the world's languages, writing automated tests, creating Wordplay examples, verifying accessibility, and teaching your peers. As a studio course, there will be no lectures, only community-based making, collaboration, coordination, and communication. Can be taken multiple times, and volunteer work can continue beyond the quarter. Raw line: Community-Driven HCI gives students the opportunity to explore how technology can be designed with local communities, while at the same time engaging broader questions in Human-Computer Interaction for Development (HCI4D), as well as notions of Autonomous Design. Students will pursue quarter-long design projects situated in Seattle. In addition to introducing participatory design methods, the course will also encourage students to think critically about issues such as equity, sustainability, and other related community-led forms of sociotechnical practice, specifically within the context of underserved or underrepresented communities both locally and abroad. [parser prerequisite filter audit] Owner id: uw-seattle-informatics Source URL: https://ischool.uw.edu/programs/informatics/curriculum Section title: The course will start with several weeks of game design basics, including mechanics, dynamics, “fun”, visual and UX design for games, narrative, user research for games, and an overview of game genres. We’ll then cover various aspects of games for social good, including game-based learning, social awareness games, social action games, and social engagement games in a variety of categories. We will also cover topics such as toxicity in multiplayer games, representation, and more. Assignments will focus on team creation more than theory and we will have a final team project where you create a game demo or prototype. Raw line: In this course students critically examine current social media platforms and then design alternative platform concepts grounded in lived experience, youth perspectives, and explicit values. Rather than treating social media as either simply harmful or simply empowering, the course asks students to identify the specific design choices, incentives, metrics, and social contexts that shape how platforms affect connection, identity, intimacy, attention, safety, belonging, and agency. Raw line: Designed for INFO majors who are interested in startups and entrepreneurship, this course will cover how new companies are founded, managed, funded, and grown. Through readings, guest speakers, activities, and case studies, students will learn what life is like as a startup founder and how to navigate the inevitable challenges that arise, as well as the mechanics of raising money for a startup through both equity and non-dilutive funding options. While the course material is applicable to many different types of startups, there will be a particular emphasis on social impact startups, SaaS companies, and online marketplaces. Developers, designers, and product managers who are thinking about launching a startup at some point in their career, as well as those interested in joining an early-stage startup after graduation, are encouraged to enroll in the course. Raw line: Examines theoretical and practical questions and challenges inherent in designing, building, deploying, maintaining, and regulating trustworthy and safe sociotechnical systems. Topics include: online governance and the emergence of Trust & Safety; an introduction to attack surfaces; reactively and proactively defining, identifying, and mitigating harms (e.g., hate speech, fraud, extremist content, child sexual abuse materials, platform manipulation); the increasing role of regulation and compliance; the challenges of “trust” and “safety” at scale; the threats and opportunities of emerging technologies; and careers in Trust & Safety. Raw line: Nonprofits frequently face constraints in budget and technical expertise, limiting their ability to implement and manage sophisticated technology solutions. These solutions could significantly enhance engagement with their beneficiaries, efficiently handle constituent management, and automate repetitive tasks. The class aims to work with a nonprofit sponsor to identify high-value workflows that can be automated and utilize no code and AI solutions for designing, developing, and testing these workflows. Our goal is to deploy these solutions and transition their management to nonprofits, unburdening them from menial tasks and freeing up resources to focus on their missions and impact. [parser prerequisite filter audit] Owner id: uw-seattle-informatics Source URL: https://ischool.uw.edu/programs/informatics/curriculum Section title: This recurring, quarterly research studio will engage you as a volunteer open source contributor that helps design, build, and maintain Wordplay, creative coding research platform for creating accessible, interactive typographic media. Our research goals are to create a global platform for creative expression with language that celebrates every individual's culture, identity, and values, while also enabling youth to learn about the power and limits of data and algorithms. Your contributions can include designing and redesigning the language and platform, implementing new user interface features, improving correctness and reliability of current features, localizing to one or more of the world's languages, writing automated tests, creating Wordplay examples, verifying accessibility, and teaching your peers. As a studio course, there will be no lectures, only community-based making, collaboration, coordination, and communication. Can be taken multiple times, and volunteer work can continue beyond the quarter. Raw line: Designed for Informatics majors who are interested in startups and entrepreneurship, this course will cover how new companies are founded, managed, funded, and grown. Through readings, guest speakers, activities, and case studies, students will learn what life is like as a startup founder and how to navigate the inevitable challenges that arise, as well as the mechanics of raising money for a startup through both equity and non-dilutive funding options. While the course material is applicable to many different types of startups, there will be a particular emphasis on social impact startups, SaaS companies, and online marketplaces. Developers, designers, and product managers who are thinking about launching a startup at some point in their career, as well as those interested in joining an early-stage startup after graduation, are encouraged to enroll in the course. [parser prerequisite filter audit] Owner id: uw-seattle-informatics Source URL: https://ischool.uw.edu/programs/informatics/curriculum Section title: This recurring, quarterly research studio will engage you as a volunteer open source contributor that helps design, build, and maintain Wordplay, creative coding research platform for creating accessible, interactive typographic media. Our research goals are to create a global platform for creative expression with language that celebrates every individual's culture, identity, and values, while also enabling youth to learn about the power and limits of data and algorithms. Your contributions can include designing and redesigning the language and platform, implementing new user interface features, improving correctness and reliability of current features, localizing to one or more of the world's languages, writing automated tests, creating Wordplay examples, verifying accessibility, and teaching your peers. As a studio course, there will be no lectures, only community-based making, collaboration, coordination, and communication. Can be taken multiple times, and volunteer work can continue beyond the quarter. The first part of the course contains deep dives into various aspects of UX design in general: UX design, UI design, Visual design, and Game design. Then, the course will focus on the practice of creating components of the game user experience including maps, inventory, onboarding, sound design, and more. We will end will topics on accessibility, mobile, and console UX design. requirementCueLines: 1-5 Credits; Credit/No Credit Grading
4 Credits, Standard Grading
4 Credits; Standard Grading
5 Credits; Standard Grading
8 Credits; Standard Grading
8 Credits: Standard Grading
All students enrolled in the Informatics major are eligible to complete Departmental Honors in the program. Learn about the requirements.

Before you are able to graduate, you are required to complete the following procedures. If you have any questions concerning this process, please contact Student Services. Learn about the Convocation ceremony .
Biomedical and Health Informatics Option: BIME 300, BIME 435, INFO 468, INFO 478.
Capstone (8-credits): Students complete a culminating experience.
Core courses (54-58 credits)
Courses required for admission (15-20 credits)
Credits; Standard Grading
Data Science Option: INFO 370, INFO 371, INFO 430, INFO 474, or other approved data science elective
Electives (12-15 credits)

Explores how information flows through the construction industry, with emphasis on the data, coordination, and contractual requirements that shape design and execution in large projects. Students examine how construction documents, subcontractor commitments, project communications, and site realities interact across the lifecycle of a project. Topics include access to proprietary and public project data, synthetic data generation, interviews with industry practitioners, and the role of AI agents in supporting collaboration between subcontractors, general contractors, and design teams. Through site visits, field observation, and applied research, students investigate how informatics can improve decision-making, communication, and compliance in construction settings.
INFO 200: Intellectual Foundations of Informatics
INFO 201: Foundational Skills for Data Science
INFO 290: Orientation to Informatics
INFO 300: Research Methods
INFO 330: Databases and Data Modeling
INFO 340: Client-Side Development
INFO 360: Design Methods
INFO 380: Product and Information Systems Management
INFO 490: Project Capstone I and INFO 491: Project Capstone II (8 credits total)
INFO 492 A: Intensive Capstone - Critical Information Research
INFO 492 A: Intensive Capstone - Developing No Code and AI Strategies and Solutions for Non-profits
INFO 492 B: Intensive Capstone - Building Nonprofit Systems with AI Development Tools
INFO 492 C: Intensive Capstone - Construction Informatics
INFO 494 A: Research Studio - Justice-Centered Programming Language Design
INFO 494 B: Research Studio - Designing Escape Rooms for Information Literacy and Resilience
INFO 495 Internship for Credit
INFO 496 Service Learning
INFO 498 A: Community-Driven HCI
INFO 498 A: Game Development for Social Good
INFO 498 A: UX for Gaming
INFO 498 B: AI, Text Reuse, and the Art of Stealing
INFO 498 B: Game Design for Social Good
INFO 498 C: Designing Social Media Futures
INFO 498 E: AI, Robots, and Transcending Religion

All students enrolled in the Informatics major are eligible to complete Departmental Honors in the program. Learn about the requirements.

Before you are able to graduate, you are required to complete the following procedures. If you have any questions concerning this process, please contact Student Services. Learn about the Convocation ceremony .
